title: Translation and Interpretation Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541930 ($2,402,956,156) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541930 ($6,131,619,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Veterinary Assistants and Laboratory Animal Caretakers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $162,427 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541930); class <5: 2,193 firms, avg revenue/firm $268,027 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,946 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 223 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,388,377 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $217,281 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,092,642 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $483,998 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 95 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,796,705 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $907,184 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,892,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,399,598 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $211,163,133 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,047,030 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 28
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: The 4-digit 5419 OEWS data is contaminated by veterinary occupations and is unusable for V — Interpreters and Translators (27-3091) show only 2.1% of wage bill, which is wildly understated. Role rebuilt from LSP W-2 wage structure per prompt instruction. Staff interpreters/translators: estimated 25% of W-2 wages (most production linguists are 1099 freelancers in COGS). Written translation (~60% of this role's work): MTPE is the default workflow; AI generates the draft, humans post-edit, at 30-50% per-word discount — high automatable fraction (0.65). On-site and live interpretation (~40% of role): physical/real-time, certified-interpreter requirements under Section 1557/court mandates; low automatable fraction (0.15). Blended: 0.60×0.65 + 0.40×0.15 = 0.39 + 0.06 = 0.45.

TMS platforms with AI orchestration automate file prep, TM/termbase management, vendor matching, QA passes, and delivery workflow — the operational core of PM work at LSPs. Client-side program management, escalation, SLA negotiation, and compliance oversight (HIPAA, court certification programs) persist as human work. Fraction 0.40: slightly under half because institutional account management is relationship-intensive and is a growing share of PM work as transactional tasks automate.

Appointment scheduling, interpreter matching, telephony routing, and OPI/VRI dispatch are already being automated end-to-end by TMS/OPI platforms. Large interpreting platforms (LanguageLine, Propio, Cloudbreak) have largely automated these functions. Residual human oversight: quality escalations, onboarding new interpreters, certified-interpreter verification. Fraction 0.75.

RFP responses, quoting, proposal generation, and CRM updates automate well (AI can draft and price bids based on volume/language-pair matrices). Healthcare-system, court, and enterprise localization relationships persist and are the firm's real asset — renewing statewide court contracts or hospital language-access agreements requires trust, compliance knowledge, and personal relationship. Fraction 0.30.

Capacity planning, margin management, and production monitoring automate substantially (BI/AI dashboards). Compliance ownership (HIPAA, DEA court interpreter certification compliance, JCAHO), strategic decisions, and board reporting persist as human work. Fraction 0.30.

Per-word/per-minute billing reconciliation (complex with freelance rate matrices), freelancer payables, AP/AR, invoicing, and compliance paperwork automate near-fully with modern financial automation. Residual human work: dispute resolution, complex multi-party billing (subcontractor cascades), government contract compliance documentation. Fraction 0.75.

pi_dist.rationale: Client relationship control is mixed by segment. Healthcare/court interpreting (estimated 40-50% of revenue in this code): institutional contracts with hospital systems, state court systems, and insurers — firm owns the contract, relationship is institutional and transferable in acquisition. However, reimbursement rates are set by the institutional payer and are revised downward at renewal when AI lowers delivery cost; per-encounter rate compression is documented. Written translation (estimated 50-60%): per-word pricing with MTPE discount tiers — AI savings are passed directly to clients as lower per-word rates; the market sets prices through competitive RFP. Enterprise localization: long-term relationships but cost-based procurement. Overall score 0.40: moderate control in interpreting (mandated/contracted), low in written translation (per-word market pricing). Pricing-model deflation is documented and immediate — MTPE at 30-50% discount is now the industry default, meaning AI savings go to clients, not operator margin.

pi_moat.rationale: Section 1557 of the ACA mandates qualified interpreters for healthcare LEP patients — critical communications using machine translation must be reviewed by qualified human translators (2025 HHS rule). State court certification requirements mandate certified interpreters in proceedings. These statutory requirements create a demand floor for qualified human interpretation that AI cannot bypass for covered encounters. Moat elements: (1) Institutional contract switching costs — health system language-access programs are 3-5 year contracts, compliance-intensive to switch; (2) TM/termbase investment — proprietary translation memories for specific clients/industries create switching costs; (3) ISO 17100-type certification requirements for regulated content (FDA submissions, legal filings); (4) Compliance ownership (HIPAA, court certification). However: written translation for general enterprise content faces direct bypass risk (LLM in-house + reviewer); per-word pricing commoditization erodes moat in non-mandated segments; AI speech translation is advancing toward remote interpretation modalities. Score 0.45: genuine statutory moat in mandate-protected segments, limited moat in substitutable written translation.
sources: Section 1557 ACA healthcare language access requirements — morganlewis.com/blogs/healthlawscan/2025/01/affordable-care-act-section-1557-new-language-accessibility-requirements

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: ALC 2024 Industry Survey found 75% of LSCs report customers inquiring about AI; 29% of MT-using companies (subset) use LLMs for MT output (up from 11% one year prior — an 18pp jump in one year). For large/mid-tier LSPs, MTPE is already the default workflow for written translation. Estimating ~45% of US LSP firms materially using AI in automatable functions (production translation, TMS orchestration) in 2026 — large firms 95%+, mid-tier 70%+, small firms (the 2,193 <5-employee firms) lagging but rapidly adopting given existential cost pressure. Historical analog: CAT tool adoption in translation took ~15 years (mid-1990s to ~2010) to reach majority. But AI/MTPE diffusion is far faster: (1) no hardware/integration cost, (2) documented 30-50% cost savings are immediate competitive necessity, (3) enterprise clients are mandating AI-augmented workflows. With 45% current adoption and ALC data showing 18pp jump in one year among MT-using LSCs, t50 from today (2026) = ~2 years (majority adoption by 2028). Plausible range: 1-4 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: ALC 2024 Industry Survey, reported by Slator
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 75% of LSCs reported customers inquiring about AI technology; 29% of the 70 companies using MT used LLMs to produce MT output (up from 11% in 2023, an 18pp increase); 16% building or have built their own MT model in-house (up from 3% in 2023). Larger LSPs already running MTPE as default workflow. Estimated 45% material adoption rate across all US LSP firms when accounting for size-weighted distribution.

historical_analogs.value: CAT (Computer-Assisted Translation) tool adoption among professional translators: starting ~mid-1990s (Trados commercialized 1995), reaching majority adoption ~2005-2010, approximately 10-15 years to 50%+. However, MT/AI adoption is at least 3-5x faster due to zero marginal cost, immediate competitive necessity, and client mandates.
historical_analogs.source: Translation industry history; Slator language industry coverage; ALC survey data
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: CAT tools took ~10-15 years to reach majority adoption (1995-2010). ALC 2024 data shows 18pp jump in LLM-for-MT adoption in a single year, suggesting AI adoption velocity is 5-10x faster.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Nimdzi 100 2024/2025 commentary; McKinsey small business ownership data; general small business owner demographics
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Nimdzi 100 notes many top 51-100 firms are 'traditionally founder-owned LSPs.' McKinsey: more than half of all small business owners are over 55. General small business: baby boomers (55-73) represent 37% of all small business owners. LSP sector has seen wave of M&A partly driven by founder aging and inability to pass company to next generation as AI disrupts the business model. Estimated 30% of LSP owners are 60+, consistent with small-service-business age distribution.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No language-services-specific succession shortage is documented by ALC, Nimdzi, or Slator. The sector faces M&A-driven consolidation (founders selling to PE) but this is driven by AI disruption making founder exits attractive, not a documented succession shortage. ALC surveys focus on AI adoption and pricing, not owner age demographics.

active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Active PE-backed consolidators doing add-ons in 2024-2025: (1) Propio Language Services (PE: Leonard Green Partners) — acquired Akorbi, ASL Services, ULG in 2024 + CyraCom in 2025; (2) Big Language Solutions (PE: MSouth Equity Partners) — active buy-and-build in regulated verticals; (3) EasyTranslate (PE: Pride Capital Partners) — acquired World Translation 2024; (4) Cloudbreak/EquitiHealth (PE: GTCR) — acquired Voyce 2024; (5) TransPerfect — 10 acquisitions in 2025 (founder-owned but operating as PE-like consolidator). Minimum 5 active platforms.

buy_mult.source: Slator M&A 2024 coverage; Delancey Street Partners LSP Sector Review; general professional services PE buyout data
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Stoquart acquired Version internationale at approximately 5x adjusted EBITDA (one disclosed comp from Slator coverage). General PE lower-middle-market professional services: 4-6x EBITDA for platform acquisitions. Healthcare interpreting (mandate-protected recurring revenue) likely commands slight premium vs. pure translation. Setting buy_mult = 5.0 EBITDA as the median estimated paid for $1-10M EBITDA LSP targets. Note: very few multiples are publicly disclosed in language services M&A.

exit_mult.source: Language services M&A market analysis; healthcare BPO comparable transactions; Slator M&A 2024 coverage
exit_mult.figure_quoted: No specific $10M+ EBITDA LSP platform exit multiple publicly disclosed. Cloudbreak/EquitiHealth (GTCR-backed, medical interpreting platform) represents a healthcare interpreting platform with mandate-protected revenue; healthcare BPO comps trade 8-12x EBITDA at platform exit. TransPerfect ($1.3B revenue) is valued at $5-7B implied, suggesting 8-10x EBITDA. For a blended LSP platform with both interpreting and translation: exit_mult estimated 8.5x (mid-range healthcare interpreting premium, offset by translation segment AI pressure). Downward valuation pressure noted in 2024 due to AI and rate environment.

pricing_structure.value: Per-word (written translation, ~50-60% of revenue); per-minute or per-encounter (interpreting, ~40-50%). Per-word MTPE standard rate: $0.05-$0.15/word (30-50% discount vs. full human translation at $0.10-$0.30/word). Interpreting: per-minute OPI/VRI at contracted per-encounter rates for healthcare; per-hour for on-site. AI savings in written translation flow entirely to clients via lower per-word rates (competitive market). Interpreting per-encounter rates are renegotiated downward at contract renewal as AI improves, but Section 1557 statutory mandate maintains volume floor.
pricing_structure.source: MTPE pricing surveys; ALC 2024 Industry Survey; Wonderful Machine analogous analysis
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: MTPE costs 30-50% less than full human translation, typically ranging $0.05-$0.15 per word. Freelancers peg MTPE at 50-60% of standard translation fees. ALC 2024: 75% of LSCs had customers inquiring about AI technology, reflecting client pressure to lower per-word rates.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 19.77pp, A = 5.0. No tension: uplift_pp far exceeds the 3pp threshold. The 19.77pp uplift reflects a high ai_replaceable_share (0.473) applied to a high labor_share (0.4178). A=5.0 reflects t50=2.0 years, consistent with documented 18pp jump in LLM adoption in a single year among ALC survey respondents. Both V and A independently validate that AI displacement is both large and fast in this industry.

terminal_value.justification: Translation and interpretation is PRESSURED, not MELTING. The critical distinction: a significant share of this code's revenue is mandate-protected by statute. Section 1557 of the ACA requires qualified human interpreters for healthcare LEP patients, and the 2025 HHS rule explicitly requires qualified human review for any critical communications using machine translation — Congress and HHS have affirmatively blocked AI-only substitution for covered healthcare encounters. State court certification requirements mandate certified human interpreters in proceedings. These statutory mandates (estimated 40-50% of the code's interpreting revenue) create a genuine demand floor that persists at mature AI. USCIS sworn/certified translation, FDA-regulated labeling, and ISO 17100-certified document translation segments similarly require human certification. However, the remaining revenue — general enterprise written translation, business document translation, non-mandated content localization — IS melting: LLMs produce this content at near-zero marginal cost, and the market evidence (MTPE at 30-50% discount, TransPerfect's declining pure-translation segment) confirms active substitution. The code does not classify as MELTING because the mandate-protected segment is large enough to sustain a truncated but real industry at mature AI. DURABLE is too optimistic given the ongoing per-word rate compression even in surviving segments.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was derived from task-level analysis of LSP role structure (rebuilt from prompt role hints due to dataset contamination) and within-role AI automatable fractions based on documented MTPE workflows, AI speech translation capabilities, and TMS automation — not from deal activity. A was derived from ALC 2024 adoption survey data (29% LLM-for-MT, 18pp one-year jump) and historical CAT tool adoption analog — not from consolidation activity. No M&A deal volume, PE platform activity, or consolidation trends were referenced for V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~50-60% per-word written translation, ~40-50% per-minute/per-encounter interpretation. AI savings in written translation flow entirely to clients: MTPE is now the default workflow at 30-50% per-word discount — the operator's production cost dropped but the price passed to clients dropped equivalently. ALC 2024 confirms 75% of LSCs face customer pressure on AI pricing. Interpreting per-encounter rates are partly mandate-protected (Section 1557 creates demand floor) but contract renewal rates are bid down as AI improves OPI/VRI delivery efficiency. pi_dist = 0.40 reflects that per-word pricing deflation hands AI savings to clients in the translation segment, partially offset by contracted/mandated rate floors in interpreting. pi_moat = 0.45 reflects genuine statutory moat in mandate-protected interpreting, limited moat in substitutable written translation.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=1: A=10.0, S≈5.05; at t50=4: A=2.5, S≈3.11. S range ~3.11-5.05 depending on t50 — a nearly 2-point swing. The rapid ALC adoption data supports t50=2 but small-firm laggards could push it to 3-4.
At buy_mult=4.0 with exit_mult=8.5: M=4.5, S≈4.33; at buy_mult=7.0 with exit_mult=8.5: M=0.857, S≈3.17. Very few disclosed comps — M is the second-largest uncertainty.

At ars=0.38: V_raw=0.1588, V=6.35, S≈3.62; at ars=0.55: V_raw=0.2298, V=9.19, S≈4.06. Primarily driven by staff interpreter/translator fraction (depends on written vs. live interpretation revenue mix, which varies by firm).

reviewer_note: Role-mix contamination is total: the 4-digit 5419 OEWS proxy is dominated by veterinary occupations (541940 is the giant sibling). 27-3091 Interpreters and Translators shows only 2.1% of the 5419 wage bill, which is unusable — rebuilt from LSP W-2 wage structure per prompt instruction. Key structural note: most production linguists (translators, interpreters) are 1099 freelancers who appear in COGS, not the W-2 wage bill. This means the dataset labor_share of 0.4178 understates the true language-labor content AI attacks: AI savings land partly in gross margin (freelance spend reduction) in addition to opex. This makes the true AI value higher than V_raw suggests — the formula conservatively scores only the W-2 channel. Segment split is critical: the 28 n_band firms (100-499 employee class, avg $40.9M revenue) are the medium-sized LSPs — these are precisely the firms currently navigating MTPE transition and either being acquired by PE consolidators or struggling with margin compression. The 15 firms in the 500+ class (avg $211M revenue) are already consolidator-owned (Propio, LanguageLine subsidiaries, etc.). M multiples are highly uncertain — Stoquart/Version internationale at 5x is the only disclosed comp found; most language services M&A does not disclose deal terms.
